food_subgroup: Sweet rice preparations / festive rice dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Zarda Pulao. FOODON_03311069 (rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (rice-based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific term for sweet saffron rice exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Zarda Pulao or sweet rice preparations as a food entity.

preparation_method: garnishing with dry fruits and nuts

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of Zarda Pulao is approximately 200 g, equivalent to one medium plate as served at an Indian restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Zarda / Sweet Rice preparation; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central basmati rice and sugar component data
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition); USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides the closest reference for sweet rice preparations. Zarda Pulao is a sugar- and ghee-enriched basmati rice dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN sweet rice / kheer rice category and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 169756, cooked white rice; FDC ID 169655, granulated sugar) to derive a composite estimate for the cooked dish at approximately 60% rice, 20% sugar, 10% ghee/fat, 10% dry fruits. Energy and macros are consistent with published values for similar Indian sweet rice preparations.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (cooked, boiled) and sugar-enriched rice preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K et al. (2021) Diabetes Care
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Zarda Pulao in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 65 based on the analogous food 'white rice, boiled' (GI ~64–72, mean ~68 per Atkinson et al. 2021) adjusted downward slightly to 65 to account for the fat content from ghee (which slows gastric emptying) and the presence of dry fruits. The added sugar (sucrose, GI ~65) does not substantially alter the estimate. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (65 × 38.5 × 2.0) / 100 = 50.1 g. This is a high-GL food per serving. gi_category is 'medium' per the ≤55 low / 56–69 medium / ≥70 high threshold rule.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~18 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Zarda Pulao is low in sodium (~36 mg per 200 g serving), making it unlikely to exacerbate hypertension from a sodium standpoint; however, the high caloric density and sugar content may contribute to weight gain, which is an indirect risk factor for elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~50 per serving) and high added sugar content (~32 g per 200 g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Zarda Pulao due to its high glycemic load (~50 per 200 g serving) and substantial added sugar (~16 g per 100 g), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size should be restricted to no more than 50–75 g and paired with protein or fibre-rich foods to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~3.1 g per 100 g) and high sugar content contributing to dyslipidaemia risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Zarda Pulao contains moderate saturated fat from ghee (~6.2 g per 200 g serving) and high added sugar, both of which can adversely affect lipid profiles and increase cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Zarda Pulao (also known as Zarda, Meethe Chawal, or Sweet Saffron Rice) is a traditional South Asian festive sweet rice dish originating from the Indian subcontinent, widely prepared across North India, Pakistan, and Bangladesh. The dish is made by cooking basmati rice until tender, then enriching it with sugar, saffron (which imparts a vivid yellow-orange colour and floral aroma), ghee (clarified butter), and whole spices such as cardamom and cloves. It is garnished generously with dry fruits and nuts including raisins, cashews, and almonds, and sometimes with khoya (reduced milk solids) or coconut. The result is a fragrant, vibrantly coloured, sticky-sweet rice preparation with a rich, buttery texture and a complex aroma from saffron and cardamom. Zarda Pulao is traditionally served at weddings, Eid celebrations, and other festive occasions across South Asia and the South Asian diaspora in Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-fat, energy-dense dish (~210 kcal per 100 g) with significant added sugar (~16 g per 100 g) and saturated fat from ghee (~3.1 g per 100 g). Its glycemic index is estimated at 65 (medium GI) with a high glycemic load (~50 per 200 g serving), making it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing blood glucose. The dish is vegetarian and gluten-free but not vegan due to ghee. It is halal-compliant. Clinical caution is warranted for diabetic and cardiovascular patients due to high sugar and saturated fat content.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Zarda Pulao?
Is Zarda Pulao good for diabetics?
What is the GI of sweet rice?
What are the ingredients in Zarda Pulao?

confidence_reason: No exact ICMR-NIN entry for Zarda Pulao exists as a named dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN sweet rice preparation data and USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked white rice, sugar, and ghee. The composite estimate is internally consistent and plausible for the described dish. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (sweet rice category) and USDA FoodData Central component data. No single approved-source entry for 'Zarda Pulao' as a named dish was found. Values reflect a typical restaurant-style preparation with approximately 60% cooked basmati rice, 20% sugar, 10% ghee, and 10% dry fruits/nuts by weight. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this food category. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based and dairy-fat dish with no significant animal protein source; confirmed effectively zero per ICMR-NIN data for similar preparations. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; ghee contains negligible industrially produced trans fats, and naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are present only in trace amounts below reporting threshold — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central ghee entry. 6. GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on analogous white rice GI data from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Zarda Pulao was found in the approved GI literature. 7. Multilingual names provided for Indian cuisine served in Singapore context: Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are all populated. 8. The dish is served at Bombay Grill (Restaurant Delivery) on field survey data at SGD 12.90.
